feedback: [Problem] User cannot unblock a blocked item in Firefox and reset to default, [Symptoms] content is blocked and inaccessible, [Environment] unknown, [Possible Category] Configuration Issue, [Problem Type] Unblock content
collected:
Block lists in Firefox

By default, Firefox uses the Disconnect.me Level 1 block list. For more aggressive tracking protection, use the Disconnect.me Level 2 block list. Level 1 blocks commonly known analytics trackers, social sharing trackers, and advertising trackers. However, the basic protection list allows some known content trackers so that fewer pages break or fail to load. Level 2 list blocks all known trackers, including analytics trackers, social sharing trackers, and advertising trackers as well as content trackers. The strict list will break some videos, photo slideshows, and social networking features. Change your block list Click the menu button The image "fx57menu" does not exist.. Click . This takes you to the Content Blocking section in your Firefox Settings Privacy & Security panel. Select the Custom radio button. Click the Change block list link. Choose the block list you want to use. Click Save Changes .

summary:
[Problem] Dark Reader toolbar button missing, [Symptoms] button disappeared from toolbar, only a few extension icons visible, [Environment] OS unknown, Firefox version unknown, [Possible Category] UI / Interaction, [Problem Type] Extension icon missing

The Dark Reader icon can vanish if the extension is disabled, hidden, or its toolbar button is accidentally removed from the address‑bar UI. In Chrome, restoring the session on startup or having a large number of extensions can cause toolbar icons to collapse into the overflow menu, while Firefox’s “Restore Previous Session” setting or a corrupted toolbar layout can hide the bookmarks toolbar (and extension icons). Similar icon‑hiding issues on Windows 11 point to taskbar or OS‑level display glitches, so the problem is likely a UI/interaction glitch in the browser’s toolbar configuration rather than a bug in Dark Reader itself.

feedback: [Problem] Unwanted pop-ups appear in lower right corner of screen, [Symptoms] Persistent notifications, [Environment] Unknown OS and Firefox version, [Possible Category] UI / Interaction, [Problem Type] Notification pop-ups
collected: [Untriaged] Flood of suspicious notifications from new Firefox version

What are pop-ups?

Pop-up windows, or pop-ups, are windows that appear automatically without your permission. They vary in size, but usually don't cover the whole screen. Some pop-ups open on top of the current Firefox window, while others appear underneath Firefox (pop-unders). Firefox allows you to control both pop-ups and pop-unders in Firefox Settings . Pop-up blocking is turned on by default, so you don't have to worry about enabling it to prevent pop-ups from appearing in Firefox. When blocking a pop-up, Firefox displays an information bar (if it hasn’t been previously dismissed – see below), as well as an icon in the address bar. When you click either the Options Preferences button in the info bar or the icon in the address bar, a menu is displayed with the following choices: Blocking pop-ups may interfere with some websites: Some websites, including some banking sites, use pop-ups for important features. Blocking all pop-ups disables such features. To allow specific websites to use pop-ups, while still blocking all others, you can add those specific websites to the list of allowed sites. Blocking pop-ups doesn't always work: Although Firefox blocks most pop-ups, some websites may show pop-ups using uncovered methods, even when they are blocked.

summary:
[Problem] Unwanted pop-ups appear in lower right corner of screen, [Symptoms] Persistent notifications, [Environment] Unknown OS and Firefox version, [Possible Category] UI / Interaction, [Problem Type] Notification pop-ups

Unwanted pop‑ups in the lower‑right corner are a common symptom of active notification services—whether they originate from a browser’s built‑in news or site‑push alerts (e.g., Chrome “News” pop‑ups), from Windows’ Action Center, or from extensions. Across multiple reports the same behavior is noted in Chrome, Windows 10, and occasionally other browsers, and it is usually controllable by disabling notifications in the browser settings, turning off related Windows services or policies, or removing offending extensions. Therefore, the bug likely stems from an enabled notification channel rather than a rendering defect, and investigation should focus on OS notification settings, browser notification permissions, and any installed add‑ons.

4. **Lack of Expected vs. Actual Detail** – The report would benefit from explicit statements of what the user expects (only pinned sites appear) versus what actually happens (any visited site appears).  

Because of these gaps, we should **search** for more data—ask the user for environment details, exact steps, screenshots, and any relevant console logs—before attempting to generate a full bug report.

summary:
[Problem] Adding Google Voice ringtone to Firefox for call notifications, [Symptoms] No ring sound when receiving calls, [Environment] OS: unknown, Firefox version: unknown, [Possible Category] Functionality, [Problem Type] Notification integration.

The issue is most likely caused by Firefox blocking Google Voice’s audio or notification permissions. Google Voice requires that the browser allows sound, WebRTC, and cookie access, and that the user is signed in with a valid Google account; recent updates have tightened these requirements. Additionally, firewalls, VPNs, or other security software can interfere with call audio, and older “custom ringtone” options have been removed in newer Google Voice releases. Ensuring Firefox is up‑to‑date, that sound and notification permissions are granted for voice.google.com, and that no network filtering blocks the call stream should restore the ringtone.

summary:
[Problem] Firefox fails to fully load after update; [Symptoms] incomplete UI and unresponsive controls; [Environment] Windows desktop with latest Firefox update; [Possible Category] Functionality; [Problem Type] UI crash

None of the retrieved documents reference Firefox directly, but they highlight a common pattern: UI components often fail to initialise after a major update when a dependency becomes incompatible or missing.  The Oracle appliance note, for example, recommends waiting a few minutes after a reboot before the UI responds, suggesting that a brief post‑update state can mask the real issue.  By analogy, the latest Firefox build on Windows may be missing a native library, corrupted binary, or conflicting extension, leading to an incomplete UI and unresponsive controls.  Reviewing Firefox’s crash logs, disabling add‑ons, and verifying the integrity of the updated binaries are therefore sensible first‑step diagnostics.

summary:
[Problem] Firefox not saving new passwords [Symptoms] old passwords are auto-filled after update [Environment] Not specified [Possible Category] Functionality [Problem Type] Password Manager Not Updating

Firefox’s password manager may be disabled or overridden by a third‑party manager or an extension, which prevents new logins from being stored while still auto‑filling existing entries.  The “Sign‑ins & Passwords” setting can be toggled off during an update, or privacy settings (e.g., “Never remember passwords”) can block saving.  Conflicting add‑ons or corrupted login data may also cause the issue.  Restoring the default password‑saving option and checking for extension conflicts or blocked storage usually resolves the problem.

Given the lack of environment details and contextual information, we cannot confidently generate a complete bug report. We need to gather more data to determine the root cause (e.g., whether it is a user setting, policy, extension conflict, or a software bug).

The next step is to verify bookmark‑related settings, system policies, or feature‑flag configurations in the affected environment.

feedback: [Problem] Email auto-completion continues after disabling password/sign-in autofill; [Symptoms] Email address automatically appears in sign-in fields on Amazon and Mozilla sites; [Environment] User hasn't specified OS or Firefox version; [Possible Category] UI / Interaction; [Problem Type] Autofill persistence
collected: [Untriaged] I get autocomplete data (email address) in forms which is not mine after firefox update

Enable/disable login autofill

At the top right, click the menu button to open the menu panel. Then, select . The Firefox about:logins page will open in a new tab. At the top right, click the Firefox logins menu and then select . The Firefox panel will open to the Logins and Passwords Passwords section. 5. Make sure the Ask to save logins and passwords for websites Ask to save passwords option is checked and check the Autofill logins and passwords Fill usernames and passwords automatically option to enable it, or uncheck to disable it. Note: Some websites have a limit on the length of the password field. If you paste or autofill input that is too long you may see a message such as: “Please shorten this text to 20 characters or less”, and will need to use a shorter password.
[Settings UI] Autofill Logins and passwords not working [maybe related to strange Enterprise Policies behaviour]
rationale:
The collected information does not yet contain all the details needed for a complete bug report. While we have the user’s description of the problem and some related notes from the bug tracker, we’re missing critical context such as:

* **Environment details** – OS version, Firefox version, and any extensions or enterprise policies that might influence autofill.
* **Reproduction steps** – A clear, step‑by‑step procedure that consistently triggers the autofill persistence issue.

summary:
[Problem] Email auto-completion continues after disabling password/sign-in autofill; [Symptoms] Email address automatically appears in sign-in fields on Amazon and Mozilla sites; [Environment] User hasn't specified OS or Firefox version; [Possible Category] UI / Interaction; [Problem Type] Autofill persistence

The issue likely stems from the way browsers handle form autofill and the persistence of credential data across pages that separate the email and password entry (e.g., Google’s two‑step sign‑in). Even after disabling password autofill, the browser may still supply an email address because the first step is considered a separate form with an “autocomplete=email” attribute, and extensions or OS‑level credential stores (Windows 11 auto‑sign‑in) can re‑inject that value. This combination of split‑page sign‑in flows, the HTML autocomplete attribute, and remaining credential caches explains why the email continues to populate in sign‑in fields on Amazon and Mozilla sites.
